html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 披萝带荔 - Metaphor for living in seclusion or poverty, often used to describe scholars or recluses.
  • 三闾氏感而为骚 - Refers to Qu Yuan, a famous poet from the State of Chu, who wrote the *Li Sao* out of personal sorrow and political frustration.
  • 牛鬼蛇神 - Term for bizarre or fantastical beings, often used in a derogatory sense to describe absurd or nonsensical things.
  • 长爪郎吟而成癖 - Refers to a person (often a scholar) who becomes obsessed with writing poetry about strange and fantastic beings.
  • 天籁 - Natural sounds or music, often used to describe spontaneous and unforced creativity.
  • 不择好音 - Means 'does not choose good sounds,' suggesting that even bad or unconventional sounds can be part of natural expression.
  • 松落落秋萤之火 - Metaphor for the author's own writing, which is small and insignificant compared to others.
  • 魑魅争光 - Refers to evil spirits competing for attention, suggesting that the author's work is overshadowed by more significant works.
  • 野马之尘 - Metaphor for the dust of the world, suggesting that the author's work is trivial and unimportant.
  • 魍魉见笑 - Refers to ghosts laughing at something, suggesting that the author's work is laughable or ridiculous.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 聊斋志异 - The title of the collection of ghost stories written by Pu Songling, meaning 'Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio.'
  • 蒲松龄 - The author of *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*, a renowned writer during the Qing dynasty.
  • 三闾氏 - Refers to Qu Yuan, a famous poet from the State of Chu during the Warring States period.
  • 干宝 - A famous historian and writer from the Jin dynasty, known for his work *Search for the Supernatural* (*搜神记*).
  • 黄州 - A place where the author may have been influenced by the works of other writers, possibly referring to Huangzhou, a place associated with the poet Su Shi.
  • 五爷衢头 - A place where rumors and gossip are spread, often used to describe a busy market or street.
  • 三生石上 - A stone that records one's past lives, often used in Buddhist contexts to suggest a connection to previous lives.

Proper Nouns

  • 聊斋志异 - The title of the collection of ghost stories.
  • 蒲松龄 - The author of *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*.
  • 三闾氏 - Qu Yuan, a famous poet from the State of Chu.
  • 干宝 - A famous historian and writer from the Jin dynasty.
  • 黄州 - A place where the author may have been influenced by other writers.
  • 五爷衢头 - A place where rumors and gossip are spread.
  • 三生石上 - A stone that records one's past lives.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 松悬弧时 - Traditional Chinese custom marking the birth of a male child.
  • 药膏如钱，圆粘乳际 - Describes a medicinal ointment shaped like a coin, stuck to the nipple area, symbolizing the child's birth.
  • 寤而松生，果符墨志 - The dream comes true when the child is born, and the child's fate aligns with the dream.
  • 少羸多病，长命不犹 - The child is frail and often ill, with a lifespan not as long as others.
  • 门庭之凄寂，则冷淡如僧 - The household is quiet and desolate, like a monk's dwelling.
  • 笔墨之耕耘，则萧条似钵 - The work of writing is sparse and solitary, like a monk's begging bowl.
  • 面壁人果吾前身耶 - A rhetorical question asking if the writer is the reincarnation of a monk who meditated facing the wall.
  • 有漏根因，未结人天之果 - Refers to the Buddhist concept of being trapped in the cycle of rebirth due to unfulfilled causes and conditions.
  • 随风荡堕，竟成藩溷之花 - The writer's fate is like a flower that falls into the mud, symbolizing a life of hardship and obscurity.
  • 茫茫六道，何可谓无其理哉 - Refers to the Buddhist concept of the six realms of existence, questioning the inevitability of such a fate.
  • 子夜荧荧，灯昏欲蕊 - Describes the dim light of a lamp at midnight, as if it were about to bloom like a flower.
  • 萧斋瑟瑟，案冷疑冰 - Describes the cold and quiet study, with a desk that feels as cold as ice.
  • 集腋为裘，妄续幽冥之录 - A metaphor for compiling small pieces of writing into a larger work, akin to sewing together fur to make a coat.
  • 浮白载笔，仅成孤愤之书 - Describes the act of writing in a state of solitude and frustration, producing a book that reflects personal grievances.
  • 惊霜寒雀，抱树无温 - Describes a bird shivering in the cold, clinging to a tree for warmth.
  • 吊月秋虫，偎栏自热 - Describes a bug seeking warmth by huddling against a railing in the autumn moonlight.
  • 知我者，其在青林黑塞间乎 - A rhetorical question asking if the writer's true understanding will be found among the green forests and dark, secluded places.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 瞿昙 - A term for Buddha, often used in Buddhist texts.
  • 六道 - The six realms of existence in Buddhist cosmology: heaven, human, asura, animal, hungry ghost, and hell.
  • 幽冥之录 - A reference to writings on the supernatural or the afterlife, often associated with ghost stories or spiritual texts.
  • 孤愤之书 - A term for a work that expresses personal grievances or frustration, often associated with literary works that reflect the author's inner turmoil.

Proper Nouns

  • 松 - The name of the person being described, likely a literary or historical figure.
  • 先大人 - Refers to the father of the person being described.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 康熙己未春日 - Specific date reference in the Chinese calendar system, combining the era name (康熙) with the sexagenary cycle (己未).
  • 柳泉自题 - Self-titled by Liuquan, indicating the text is a self-titled work by the author Liuquan.
  • 卷一 考城隍 - Chapter One: Examination of the City God. '考城隍' can be interpreted as an examination or inspection of the city god, possibly referring to a ritual or a story about the city god.
  • 耳中人 - People in the ear, possibly a metaphor for hearing or a supernatural being.
  • 尸变 - Corpse transformation, referring to a corpse coming to life or changing in some way, often in a supernatural context.
  • 喷水 - Spitting water, describing a phenomenon where water is expelled or sprayed, possibly in a supernatural or magical context.
  • 瞳人语 - Speaking through the pupil, suggesting a person or entity communicating through their eyes, possibly a supernatural ability.
  • 画壁 - Painted wall, possibly with supernatural or symbolic significance.
  • 山魈 - Mountain demon, referring to a mythical creature or demon from the mountains.
  • 咬鬼 - Biting ghosts, possibly in a supernatural context.
  • 捉狐 - Catching foxes, possibly in a supernatural or magical context.
  • 荞中怪 - Strange things in buckwheat, possibly involving unusual or supernatural occurrences.
  • 宅妖 - House spirits, referring to supernatural entities or spirits that inhabit a house.
  • 王六郎 - Proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.
  • 偷桃 - Stealing peaches, possibly in a supernatural or magical context.
  • 种梨 - Planting pears, possibly in a supernatural or magical context.
  • 劳山道士 - Lao Mountain Taoist, referring to a Taoist from Lao Mountain, possibly a character in the story.
  • 长清僧 - Changqing monk, referring to a monk from Changqing, possibly a character in the story.
  • 蛇人 - Snake person, possibly a person with a snake-like appearance or associated with snakes.
  • 斫蟒 - Chopping pythons, possibly in a supernatural or magical context.
  • 犬奸 - Dog adultery, possibly in a supernatural or magical context.
  • 雹神 - Hail god, referring to a deity associated with hail.
  • 狐嫁女 - Fox marrying a daughter, possibly a fox marrying a human or another supernatural being.
  • 娇娜 - Proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.
  • 僧孽 - Monk's sin, possibly in a supernatural or moral context.
  • 妖术 - Supernatural arts, referring to magical or supernatural abilities.
  • 野狗 - Wild dogs, possibly in a supernatural or magical context.
  • 三生 - Three lives, possibly referring to three lifetimes or reincarnations.
  • 狐入瓶 - Fox entering a bottle, possibly in a supernatural or magical context.
  • 鬼哭 - Ghost crying, possibly in a supernatural context.
  • 真定女 - True Defining Woman, possibly referring to a woman with a defining or supernatural quality.
  • 焦螟 - Proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.
  • 叶生 - Proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.
  • 四十千 - Forty thousand, possibly in a supernatural or magical context.
  • 成仙 - Becoming an immortal, referring to achieving immortality or becoming a divine being.
  • 新郎 - Bridegroom, referring to a man who is getting married.
  • 灵官 - Spiritual official, referring to a deity or official associated with spiritual matters.
  • 王兰 - Proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.
  • 鹰虎神 - Eagle Tiger God, referring to a deity associated with eagles and tigers.
  • 王成 - Proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.
  • 青凤 - Proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.
  • 画皮 - Painted skin, possibly in a supernatural or magical context.
  • 贾儿 - Proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.
  • 蛇癖 - Snake obsession, possibly in a supernatural or magical context.
  • 考城隍 - Examination of the City God, possibly referring to an examination or inspection of the city god, possibly in a ritual or story context.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 康熙己未 - The year Jiwei during the reign of Emperor Kangxi (1665-1666), providing a historical context for the text.
  • 柳泉 - The author's self-title, possibly referring to a place or a personal name.
  • 城隍 - The city god, a deity associated with the protection and governance of a city.
  • 山魈 - A mythical creature or demon from the mountains, often associated with mountains and forests.
  • 劳山 - Lao Mountain, a famous Taoist mountain in Shandong Province, known for its Taoist temples and legends.
  • 长清 - Changqing, a place name, possibly referring to a location in Shandong Province.
  • 真定 - A place name, possibly referring to a location in Hebei Province.
  • 焦螟 - A proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.
  • 叶生 - A proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.
  • 王兰 - A proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.
  • 王成 - A proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.
  • 青凤 - A proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.
  • 贾儿 - A proper name, possibly referring to a character in the story.

Proper Nouns

  • 康熙 - The era name of Emperor Kangxi.
  • 柳泉 - The author's self-title.
  • 城隍 - The city god.
  • 山魈 - A mythical creature or demon from the mountains.
  • 劳山 - Lao Mountain.
  • 长清 - Changqing.
  • 真定 - A place name.
  • 焦螟 - A proper name.
  • 叶生 - A proper name.
  • 王兰 - A proper name.
  • 王成 - A proper name.
  • 青凤 - A proper name.
  • 贾儿 - A proper name.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 檐下设几、墩各二 - Literal description of setting up tables and benches under the eaves; '檐下' (under the eaves) is a common classical expression for a place of gathering or discussion.
  • 先有一秀才坐其末 - Means 'there was already a scholar sitting at the end'; '其末' (at the end) indicates a specific position in a sequence.
  • 公便与连肩 - Means 'the official sat next to him'; '连肩' (next to) is a colloquial way of describing close proximity.
  • 几上各有笔札 - Means 'each table had pens and paper'; '笔札' (pens and paper) is a common term for writing materials.
  • 俄题纸飞下 - Means 'soon, a paper with a question floated down'; '俄' (soon) and '飞下' (floated down) describe the sudden appearance of the paper.
  • 视之有八字 - Means 'when he looked, there were eight characters'; '视之' (when he looked) is a formal way of describing the action of seeing.
  • 有心无心 - A philosophical or moral question about intention and action; '有心无心' (with heart or without heart) is often used in classical texts to discuss the nature of intention.
  • 文成，呈殿上 - Means 'the essay was completed and presented to the hall'; '呈殿上' (presented to the hall) indicates the formal submission of the work.
  • 有心为善，虽善不赏 - Means 'if one acts with good intentions, even if it is good, it will not be rewarded'; a philosophical statement about the nature of good deeds and their recognition.
  • 无心为恶，虽恶不罚 - Means 'if one acts without bad intentions, even if it is bad, it will not be punished'; a philosophical statement about the nature of bad deeds and their consequences.
  • 诸神传赞不已 - Means 'the gods praised him endlessly'; '传赞不已' (praised endlessly) emphasizes the continuous and enthusiastic praise.
  • 召公上 - Means 'the gods summoned the official'; '召公上' (summoned the official) indicates a formal call to action.
  • 谕曰 - Means 'said' or 'instructed'; '谕曰' (said/instructed) is a formal way of describing a divine command.
  • 河南缺一城隍 - Means 'there is a vacancy for a city god in Henan'; '城隍' (city god) refers to a local deity responsible for protecting the city.
  • 辱膺宠命，何敢多辞 - Means 'I am honored by this command, how dare I refuse?'; '辱膺宠命' (honored by this command) is a humble way of accepting a high honor.
  • 但老母七旬，奉养无人，请得终其天年，惟听录用 - Means 'but my mother is seventy years old, and there is no one to care for her, please allow me to finish her natural lifespan, and then I will accept the appointment'; '终其天年' (finish her natural lifespan) emphasizes the desire to fulfill filial duties.
  • 上一帝王像者 - Means 'the one with a picture of a king'; '帝王像' (picture of a king) refers to a depiction of a ruler.
  • 即命稽母寿籍 - Means 'he immediately ordered the check of my mother's life register'; '稽母寿籍' (check of my mother's life register) refers to verifying the lifespan of the official's mother.
  • 有长须吏捧册翻阅一过 - Means 'a bearded official held a book and quickly flipped through it'; '翻阅一过' (quickly flipped through) indicates a quick review.
  • 白：“有阳算九年。” - Means 'he reported: 'There are nine years of yang calculation.'; '阳算九年' (nine years of yang calculation) refers to the remaining lifespan of the official's mother.
  • 不妨令张生摄篆九年，瓜代可也 - Means 'you can let Zhang take over for nine years, and then he can be replaced'; '摄篆' (take over) and '瓜代' (be replaced) are terms used in administrative contexts.
  • 应即赴任 - Means 'you should immediately take up the position'; '赴任' (take up the position) is a formal term for assuming a new role.
  • 给假九年 - Means 'grant nine years of leave'; '给假' (grant leave) indicates a period of absence.
  • 及期当复相召 - Means 'at the end of the period, you will be called back'; '及期' (at the end of the period) and '复相召' (called back) indicate a future recall.
  • 又勉励秀才数语 - Means 'he also gave the scholar some words of encouragement'; '勉励' (encourage) and '数语' (some words) indicate a brief speech.
  • 二公稽首并下 - Means 'the two officials bowed and left'; '稽首' (bowed) is a formal gesture of respect.
  • 握手，送诸郊野 - Means 'he shook hands and sent them to the outskirts'; '送诸郊野' (sent them to the outskirts) indicates a formal farewell.
  • 自言长山张某 - Means 'he introduced himself as Zhang from Changshan'; '自言' (introduced himself) and '长山张某' (Zhang from Changshan) provide the name and origin of the person.
  • 以诗赠别 - Means 'he gave a poem as a farewell gift'; '赠别' (farewell gift) indicates a parting gesture.
  • 都忘其词 - Means 'he forgot the words'; '忘其词' (forgot the words) indicates a lapse in memory.
  • 有花有酒春常在，无烛无灯夜自明 - A poetic line that means 'with flowers and wine, spring always stays; without candles or lamps, the night remains bright'; '有花有酒春常在' (with flowers and wine, spring always stays) and '无烛无灯夜自明' (without candles or lamps, the night remains bright) are metaphorical expressions about the enduring nature of certain qualities.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 城隍 - Refers to a local deity responsible for protecting the city; the concept of a city god is deeply rooted in Chinese folk religion and local traditions.
  • 帝王像 - Refers to a depiction of a ruler; this could be a reference to a specific historical figure or a generic representation of a king.
  • 长山张某 - Zhang from Changshan; Changshan is a place name, and Zhang is a common surname; this reference provides a specific identity for the scholar.
  • 有花有酒春常在，无烛无灯夜自明 - A poetic line that reflects the cultural appreciation of nature and the enduring qualities of certain elements; this line is often used in classical poetry to convey a sense of timeless beauty and resilience.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence '檐下设几、墩各二，先有一秀才坐其末，公便与连肩' describes the setting and the initial arrangement of the officials and the scholar; '其末' (at the end) indicates a specific position.
  • The sequence '俄题纸飞下，视之有八字，云：“一人二人，有心无心。”' describes the sudden appearance of a paper with a question; '俄' (soon) and '飞下' (floated down) describe the suddenness of the event.
  • The philosophical statements '有心为善，虽善不赏。无心为恶，虽恶不罚。' are linked by the common theme of intention and action; '虽善不赏' (even if it is good, it will not be rewarded) and '虽恶不罚' (even if it is bad, it will not be punished) convey the moral implications of actions.
  • The phrase '诸神传赞不已' indicates the continuous praise of the gods; '传赞不已' (praised endlessly) emphasizes the enthusiasm and duration of the praise.
  • The sequence '召公上，谕曰' describes the divine command to the official; '召公上' (summoned the official) and '谕曰' (said/instructed) indicate a formal and authoritative command.
  • The phrase '河南缺一城隍，君称其职' indicates the vacancy for a city god and the official's suitability for the role; '河南缺一城隍' (there is a vacancy for a city god in Henan) and '君称其职' (you are suitable for the role) indicate the official's qualifications.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 公既骑，乃别而去 - Respectful title 'Mr.' is used, '既骑' means 'having mounted,' and '乃别而去' means 'then parted and left.'
  • 豁若梦寤 - '豁若' means 'as if suddenly enlightened' or 'as if suddenly awakened,' and '梦寤' means 'awakening from a dream.'
  • 时卒已三日 - '时卒' means 'the time of death,' and '已三日' means 'had been three days.'
  • 母闻棺中呻吟 - '母闻' means 'the mother heard,' and '棺中呻吟' means 'groaning from within the coffin.'
  • 半日始能语 - '半日' means 'half a day,' and '始能语' means 'could finally speak.'
  • 问之长山，果有张生于是日死矣 - '问之长山' means 'asked in Changshan,' and '果有张生于是日死矣' means 'indeed, there was an Zhang Sheng who died on that day.'
  • 后九年，母果卒 - '后九年' means 'nine years later,' and '母果卒' means 'the mother indeed passed away.'
  • 营葬既毕，浣濯入室而没 - '营葬' means 'arranging the funeral,' '既毕' means 'after it was completed,' '浣濯' means 'washing,' and '入室而没' means 'entered the room and disappeared.'
  • 其岳家居城中西门里 - '其岳家' means 'her husband's family,' and '居城中西门里' means 'lived in the western gate area of the city.'
  • 忽见公镂膺朱幩，舆马甚众 - '忽见' means 'suddenly saw,' '镂膺朱幩' means 'carved chest and red banners,' and '舆马甚众' means 'many carriages and horses.'
  • 登其堂，一拜而行 - '登其堂' means 'entered their hall,' and '一拜而行' means 'bowed once and left.'
  • 相共惊疑，不知其为神 - '相共' means 'together,' '惊疑' means 'surprised and doubtful,' and '不知其为神' means 'did not know that it was a spirit.'
  • 奔询乡中，则已殁矣 - '奔询乡中' means 'rushed to ask in the village,' and '则已殁矣' means 'but he had already died.'
  • 公有自记小传，惜乱后无存，此其略耳 - '公有自记小传' means 'Mr. had a self-written biography,' '惜乱后无存' means 'unfortunately, it was lost after the chaos,' and '此其略耳' means 'this is just a brief summary.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 公 - Refers to a respected individual, likely a government official or scholar.
  • 长山 - A place name, possibly a town or region.
  • 张生 - A person's name, likely a contemporary or acquaintance.
  • 岳家 - Refers to the husband's family, a term used in traditional Chinese family relationships.
  • 城中西门里 - Refers to a specific location within a city, likely an important or affluent area.
  • 镂膺朱幩 - Describes a carriage with a carved chest and red banners, indicating a high-status vehicle.
  • 舆马 - Refers to carriages and horses, symbols of wealth and status.

Proper Nouns

  • 公 - A respectful title for a man, likely a government official or scholar.
  • 长山 - A place name, possibly a town or region.
  • 张生 - A person's name, likely a contemporary or acquaintance.
  • 岳家 - Refers to the husband's family.
  • 城中西门里 - A specific location within a city, likely an important or affluent area.
  • 镂膺朱幩 - Describes a carriage with a carved chest and red banners.
  • 舆马 - Refers to carriages and horses.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 耳中人 - Literal descriptive phrase meaning 'a person in the ear,' used to describe a supernatural or auditory hallucination.
  • 笃信导引之术 - Means 'firmly believes in the practice of guiding and leading techniques,' referring to a form of spiritual or physical exercise.
  • 寒暑不辍 - Means 'unceasing through cold and heat,' indicating continuous practice regardless of weather.
  • 若有所得 - Means 'as if he had gained something,' suggesting a sense of achievement or enlightenment.
  • 趺坐 - Means 'to sit cross-legged,' a common posture for meditation or spiritual practice.
  • 丹将成 - Means 'the elixir is about to be completed,' referring to a spiritual or alchemical achievement.
  • 颠疾 - Means 'madness' or 'insanity,' indicating a mental disorder.
  • 尸变 - Means 'transformation of a corpse,' referring to a supernatural or ghostly phenomenon.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 谭晋玄，邑诸生也 - Refers to Tan Jinxuan, a scholar from the local area. The term '邑诸生' indicates a local scholar or student.
  • 导引之术 - Refers to a form of traditional Chinese spiritual or physical exercise, similar to qigong or tai chi.
  • 阳信某翁者，邑之蔡店人 - Refers to an unnamed old man from Caidian Village in Yangxin County. The term '邑' indicates the local area.
  • 临路店宿行商 - Means 'a roadside inn for traveling merchants,' describing a place where merchants stay overnight.
  • 车夫数人，往来负贩，辄寓其家 - Refers to several cart drivers who frequently stay at the old man's inn while traveling with goods.

Proper Nouns

  • 谭晋玄 - The name of the scholar-practitioner.
  • 阳信某翁 - The name of the old man from Caidian Village in Yangxin County.
  • 蔡店 - The name of the village where the old man lives.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一日昏暮 - Specific day at dusk, setting the time and mood for the narrative.
  • 望门投止 - To knock on doors and seek shelter, describing the act of seeking refuge or lodging.
  • 坚请容纳 - Firmly requested accommodation, indicating the guests' persistence.
  • 翁沉吟 - The old man pondered, describing his thoughtful consideration.
  • 似恐不当客意 - Seemed afraid it would not suit the guests' wishes, indicating the old man's concern about the guests' satisfaction.
  • 但求一席厦宇 - Only seeking a place to stay, emphasizing the guests' modest request.
  • 更不敢有所择 - And dared not make any choices, indicating the guests' willingness to accept whatever was offered.
  • 停尸室中 - The body was kept in the room, describing the state of the deceased.
  • 子出购材木未归 - The son went out to buy wood and had not returned, providing context for the old man's decision.
  • 灵所室寂 - The room where the spirit was kept was quiet, describing the atmosphere of the room.
  • 穿衢导客往 - Led the guests through the streets, indicating the old man's guidance.
  • 灯昏案上 - The lamp was dim on the table, setting the scene with a dimly lit room.
  • 案后有搭帐 - Behind the table was a tent, describing the arrangement of the room.
  • 衣纸衾覆逝者 - The corpse was covered with a paper shroud, describing the state of the deceased.
  • 复室中有连榻 - There was a connected bed in the inner room, describing the sleeping arrangement.
  • 甫就枕，鼻息渐粗 - Just as they lay down, their breathing became heavy, describing the guests' initial state of rest.
  • 朦胧 - Drowsy or half-asleep, describing the state of one of the guests.
  • 察察有声 - A rustling sound, indicating a subtle noise.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 皆曰：'相公醉矣。' This phrase is straightforward but can be read as a collective statement or a single person's observation.
  • 居无何，闻新郎告行，笙乐暴作，纷纷下楼而去。 This sequence of events must be read as a series of actions happening in quick succession.
  • 已而主人敛酒具，小一爵，冥搜不得。 The sentence structure is elliptical, and the meaning of '小一爵' is somewhat ambiguous.
  • 或窃议卧客。翁急戒勿语，惟恐公闻。 The sentence structure is somewhat elliptical, and the sequence of events must be read carefully to understand the urgency.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 辰后 - Indicates a specific time in the Chinese time system, which is based on the twelve earthly branches.

  • 囹圄 - A term used in ancient China to describe prisons, often associated with harsh conditions.
  • 叩阙 - A formal act of petitioning the emperor, often used in cases of injustice or grievance.
  • 重犴 - Refers to serious crimes or punishments, often associated with the legal system in ancient China.
  • 乌用友也 - A rhetorical question that emphasizes the importance of friendship and loyalty.
  • 着部院审奏 - Refers to the process of sending a case to a higher authority for review and reporting, a common practice in the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 诬服论辟 - Refers to the legal process where a person is wrongfully convicted and sentenced to death, a serious miscarriage of justice.
  • 复提躬谳 - Refers to the re-examination of a case by a higher authority, often the emperor or a high-ranking official.
  • 朦胧题免 - Refers to the practice of pardoning someone with a vague or ambiguous justification, often used to avoid direct responsibility.
  • 枉法拟流 - Refers to the sentencing of someone to exile for perverting the law, a serious legal offense.

  • 宰 - Refers to the official who sentenced Huang to exile.

  • This sentence describes a sequence of events: the protagonist is leaning on a table, a ghost enters, and the ghost stares at the protagonist. The order and timing must be clear.
  • This sentence describes the protagonist's reaction and the ghost's subsequent departure. The interaction must be conveyed clearly.
  • This sentence describes the protagonist's actions and the sound of something opening. The timing and sequence must be clear.
  • This sentence describes the protagonist's discovery and the subsequent appearance of a figure. The sequence of events must be maintained.

  • 少懈，则气氤氲自缝中出 - "少懈" means a little relaxation, and "则气氤氲自缝中出" means then the gas would rise from the seams.
  • 乃裂绺帛急束之 - "乃裂绺帛" means then tearing strips of cloth, and "急束之" means tying them tightly.
  • 以手抚尸，渐温，覆以衾裯 - "以手抚尸" means touching the body with hands, "渐温" means gradually warming up, and "覆以衾裯" means covering it with quilts and sheets.
  • 中夜启视，有鼻息矣 - "中夜启视" means opening the eyes in the middle of the night, and "有鼻息矣" means there is breathing.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 庙中 - Refers to a temple, which is a place of worship in Chinese culture.
  • 家人伫望，无敢近者 - Indicates the family's fear and respect for the situation, reflecting traditional Chinese values of filial piety and respect for the dead.
  • 陈抱尸收肠，且理且哭 - Describes the act of handling a body, which is a sensitive and emotional task in Chinese culture.
  • 热气腾蒸如烟然 - Describes the appearance of the heart, which is a vivid and symbolic image.
  • 异史氏曰 - Refers to the historian or narrator, often used in classical Chinese literature to provide commentary or moral lessons.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 楚 - Refers to the state of Chu, one of the major states during the Warring States period.
  • 狐 - In Chinese folklore, foxes are often associated with supernatural beings or spirits, sometimes benevolent and sometimes malevolent.
  • 疱媪 - Refers to a kitchen woman, a common term for a female servant in ancient Chinese households.
  • 梦与人交 - In Chinese culture, dreams involving sexual encounters with spirits or animals are often interpreted as supernatural occurrences or omens.
  • 喃喃如梦语 - Describes a state of muttering or speaking incoherently, often associated with being in a trance or under the influence of a spirit.

Proper Nouns

  • 疱媪 - The kitchen woman.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 辄言不自知 - '辄' means 'always,' '言' means 'said,' and '不自知' means 'did not know.' The phrase indicates that Dong always said he did not know.
  • 久之，面目益支离 - '久之' means 'after a long time,' '面目' means 'face,' '益' means 'increasingly,' and '支离' means 'disfigured.' The phrase indicates that after a long time, Dong's face became increasingly disfigured.

  • 董大哭不去，医不得已，为之针手灸脐 - '董大哭不去' means 'Dong cried loudly and did not leave,' '医不得已' means 'the doctor had no choice but to,' '为之针手灸脐' means 'to prick his hand and burn his navel.'   • 日月至焉而已 - He would come once a month or so. '日月' is a metaphor for time, and '焉而已' means 'only' or 'just.'
  • 又一夕来谓夫人曰 - One night, he came and said to the wife. '又一夕' means 'one night again.'
  • 承帝命为太华卿 - I have been given the imperial command to be the Chief of Mount Taihua. '太华卿' is a title, possibly referring to a high-ranking official.
  • 行将远赴，事烦途隔 - I am about to travel far away, and my duties and distance will prevent me from coming. '行将' means about to, and '途隔' means separated by distance.
  • 母子持之哭 - The mother and son held him and cried. '持之' means to hold or restrain.
  • 勿尔 - Do not do that. '尔' is a particle used to emphasize a command.
  • 儿已成立 - The child has grown up. '成立' means to have grown up or become established.
  • 家计尚可存活 - The family can still survive. '家计' refers to the family's livelihood.
  • 岂有百岁不拆之鸾凤耶 - How could there be a pair of phoenixes that never part for a hundred years? '鸾凤' is a metaphor for a loving couple, and '不拆' means not to separate.
  • 好为人，勿堕父业 - Be good to others, do not fall into the ways of your father. '堕父业' means to fall into the same mistakes as one's father.
  • 十年后一相见耳 - We will meet again in ten years. '一相见耳' means 'only one more meeting.'
  • 径出门去 - He walked straight out the door. '径' means directly or straight.
  • 于是遂绝 - And so he was gone forever. '遂绝' means to finally depart or be gone.

  • 太华卿 - A high-ranking official title, possibly referring to a position related to Mount Taihua (Mount Tai).
  • 鸾凤 - A metaphor for a loving couple, often used in classical literature to describe a harmonious relationship.
  • 百岁不拆 - A reference to the mythological phoenixes that never separate, symbolizing eternal love and fidelity.
  • 家计 - The family's livelihood or financial situation.

  • 言之恐致骇怪 - '言之' means 'to speak about it,' '恐' means 'fear,' and '致骇怪' means 'to cause alarm or surprise,' suggesting that the speaker fears that revealing the truth might lead to shock or disbelief.
  • 直告或无妨乎 - '直告' means 'to tell the truth directly,' '或' means 'perhaps,' and '无妨乎' means 'it would not be harmful,' suggesting that the speaker is asking if it would be safe to reveal the truth.
  • 九泉辄为悼恨 - '九泉' means 'the underworld' or 'the netherworld,' and '辄为悼恨' means 'always feels sorrow and resentment,' indicating that the mother's spirit is always sad and resentful.

  • 酒胾不时馈送 - Phrase meaning 'to send food and drink regularly,' indicating consistent and generous provision.
  • 小阜祝母矣 - Phrase meaning 'to provide for Zhub's mother,' where '小阜' (xiǎo fù) means 'to provide for' or 'to support.'
  • 招归宁 - Phrase meaning 'to invite her to return home for a visit,' where '归宁' (guī níng) refers to a married woman visiting her parents' home.
  • 家中无人，宜早送儿还 - Phrase meaning 'there is no one at home, so it is best to send you back early,' indicating a concern for the safety and well-being of the person being addressed.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鬼 - In Chinese culture, 'ghost' (鬼) often refers to the spirit of a deceased person, sometimes with connotations of haunting or lingering.
  • 茶媪 - A tea woman, often an older woman who prepares and serves tea, a common figure in traditional Chinese households.
  • 卖浆者 - A vendor of drinks or refreshments, a common profession in traditional Chinese society.
  • 拜岳 - The act of paying respects to one's parents-in-law, a significant ritual in Chinese family life.
  • 归宁 - The practice of a married woman visiting her parents' home, often a joyous occasion in Chinese culture.
  • 小阜 - The act of providing for or supporting someone, often used in the context of family obligations.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 人已鬼，又何厌贫 - This sentence is elliptical and can be read as 'Since the person has become a ghost, why should I be afraid of poverty?' The phrase '又何厌贫' is a rhetorical question emphasizing the speaker's acceptance of poverty.
  • 祝郎母子，情意拳拳，儿固已安之矣 - This sentence is a compound structure where '祝郎母子' (Zhub's mother and son) are the subjects of the affection, and '情意拳拳' describes their deep feelings. '儿固已安之矣' is a subordinate clause expressing the speaker's acceptance of the situation.
  • 茶媪谁也 - This is a straightforward question, but the context suggests that the speaker is seeking information about the tea woman's identity.
  • 自惭不能惑行人，故求儿助之耳 - This sentence is a complex structure where '自惭不能惑行人' (ashamed that she could not entice passersby) is the reason for the action, and '故求儿助之耳' (so she asked the child for help) is the result.
  • 生于郡城卖浆者之家 - This phrase is a descriptive clause providing information about the tea woman's background.
  • 婿矣，而不拜岳，妾复何心 - This sentence is a compound structure where '婿矣' (I am now your son-in-law) and '而不拜岳' (but I have not yet paid respects to your parents-in-law) are the main clauses, and '妾复何心' (what else could I possibly feel) is a rhetorical question expressing the speaker's acceptance.

  • 代母执炊供客 - This is a compound structure where '代母' (on behalf of her mother) and '执炊供客' (cook and serve guests) are the main actions.
  • 怆心 - This is a single-word expression indicating a strong emotional reaction.
  • 遣两婢来，为之服役 - This is a compound structure where '遣两婢来' (send two maidservants to come) and '为之服役' (serve her) are the main actions.

  • 招归宁 - This is a simple action verb indicating the invitation to return home.
  • 家中无人，宜早送儿还 - This is a compound structure where '家中无人' (there is no one at home) and '宜早送儿还' (it is best to send you back early) are the main clauses.
  • 或故稽之，则飘然自归 - This is a conditional structure where '或故稽之' (if they deliberately delay) and '则飘然自归' (then they will return on their own) are the main clauses.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 細雨 - Gentle rain is a common motif in Chinese poetry, often symbolizing a peaceful or melancholic mood.

  • 紅繡鞋兒占鬼卦 - The use of red embroidered shoes for divination is a folk practice, often associated with superstition and playfulness.
  • 市井之谣 - Folk songs from the common people are often considered less refined than classical poetry, but they can also be a source of entertainment and social commentary.
  • 效颦 - The story of Xi Shi and the woman who tried to imitate her frown is a well-known anecdote in Chinese literature, illustrating the dangers of superficial imitation.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '听蕉声，一阵一阵细雨下' sets the scene with a gentle rain, creating a serene and contemplative atmosphere.
  • The rhetorical question '何处与人闲磕牙？' introduces the idea of idle chatter, which is then contrasted with the protagonist's emotional state.
  • The sequence '望穿秋水，不见还家，潸潸泪似麻' describes the protagonist's intense longing and the resulting emotional distress.
  • The repetition '又是想他，又是恨他' emphasizes the conflicting emotions of longing and frustration.
  • The action '手拿着红绣鞋儿占鬼卦' is a metaphorical expression of the protagonist's attempt to find meaning or solace through superstition.
  • The phrase '此市井之谣，有污君听' acknowledges the lowbrow nature of the folk song but also suggests that it has some value.
  • The explanation '然因流俗所尚，姑效颦耳' provides context for the protagonist's decision to imitate the folk song, despite its lowbrow nature.
  • The adjectives '音声靡靡，风度狎亵' describe the seductive and provocative nature of the protagonist's performance.
  • The phrase '士人摇惑，若不自禁' describes the effect of the protagonist's performance on the audience, suggesting a loss of control.
  • The sequence '伪醉离席，士人亦起，从之而去' describes the protagonist's departure and the audience's reaction.
  • The phrase '久之不至' indicates a delay or absence, adding to the tension.

  • The phrase '辗转无以自主，因起而觇之' describes the protagonist's indecision and eventual decision to investigate.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 即启笥出之。儿曰：“姊命我陈旧闺中。” - This sequence describes a quick action followed by a statement, which must be read together to understand the context.
  • 忽梦惠幞头紫帔来相望，言笑犹如平生。 - This sentence describes a dream that feels real, with the dream and its realism being closely linked.
  • 我今异物，父母觌面，不啻河山。 - This sentence uses a metaphor to emphasize emotional distance, which must be read as a single thought.
  • 将借妹子与家人共语，勿须惊恐。 - This sentence is a conditional statement that must be read together to understand the request and reassurance.
  • 小惠与我婶别几年矣，顿髪髪白发生！ - This sentence describes a sudden change in appearance, which must be read as a single event.

  • 捉绷席而视之 - Means 'to take the cloth and look at it,' referring to the act of examining something.
  • 丰颐而广额 - Describes a face with a full cheek and broad forehead, indicating a handsome or well-proportioned appearance.
  • 伶仃一身 - Means 'alone and destitute,' describing someone who is solitary and in need of support.
  • 将焉所托 - Means 'where to entrust or rely on,' indicating a need for support or care.
  • 俟夜无人 - Means 'wait until night when there is no one around,' suggesting a private or secretive arrangement.
  • 窃共异之 - Means 'secretly find it strange,' indicating a sense of surprise or curiosity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 聘之不可 - Refers to the traditional practice of arranged marriages in Chinese culture, where marriage was often arranged by families.
  • 母笑曰 - The mother's laughter suggests a sense of amusement or relief, possibly at the situation's irony or the daughter's unexpected behavior.
  • 区区隐衷 - The phrase reflects the cultural emphasis on modesty and the concealment of personal feelings or secrets.
  • 俟夜无人 - The suggestion to wait until night when no one is around reflects a cultural context where private matters were often discussed in secrecy.

[205]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 更数夕，夜将半 - Indicates the passage of several nights, approaching midnight.
  • 养母之德，刻刻不去诸怀 - Figurative expression meaning 'the virtue of raising a mother is constantly in my heart.'
  • 可一而不可再 - Proverbial phrase meaning 'can be done once but not twice,' emphasizing the uniqueness of an opportunity or action.
  • 为君贫不能婚，将为君延一线之续 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'because you are poor and cannot marry, I will extend a thread of hope for you.'
  • 一索而得 - Figurative expression meaning 'to find something easily or quickly.'
  • 信水复来 - Metaphorical expression referring to a recurrence of difficult circumstances.
  • 破戒而再 - Phrase meaning 'to break a vow or rule and do it again,' often used in a religious or moral context.
  • 囊中何物 - Direct question meaning 'What is in the bag?'
  • 仇人头耳 - Literal description meaning 'the head of an enemy.'
  • 向不与君言者，以机事不密，惧有宣泄 - Figurative expression meaning 'I did not tell you before because the matter was secret and I feared it would be revealed.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 养母 - Refers to the act of raising a mother, emphasizing filial piety and maternal care.
  • 床第 - Euphemism for sexual relations or the marital bed.
  • 浙人 - Refers to someone from Zhejiang, a province in southeastern China.
  • 司马 - Ancient Chinese military rank, often translated as 'marshal' or 'general.'
  • 陷于仇 - Refers to being captured or betrayed by an enemy.
  • 籍吾家 - Phrase meaning 'to enslave or register my family,' indicating a severe form of punishment or subjugation.

Proper Nouns

  • 異史氏 - A title used by Pu Songling in his *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio* to introduce a commentary or moral.
  • 舉進士 - A term referring to passing the imperial examination to become a Jinshi (advanced scholar).
  • 奉祖母以終老 - A term referring to honoring one's grandmother until the end of one's life.

[207]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 耽飲 - Literally means 'to indulge in drinking,' indicating a strong fondness or addiction to alcohol.
  • 浮三白 - Refers to a custom of drinking three cups of wine before going to bed, often associated with a specific ritual or practice.
  • 床頭樽常不空 - Literally means 'the wine jar by the bed is never empty,' indicating a constant supply of wine.
  • 似有人共臥者 - Literally means 'it seems like someone is sharing the bed,' suggesting an unusual presence.
  • 覆裳堕耳 - Literally means 'the coverlet has fallen,' implying that the sensation was due to the coverlet falling off.
  • 似猫而巨 - Literally means 'like a cat but large,' describing the size and appearance of the creature.
  • 酣醉而大卧 - Literally means 'deeply drunk and lying down,' indicating the state of the creature.
  • 此我酒友也 - Literally means 'this is my drinking companion,' expressing the narrator's recognition of the creature.
  • 覆衣加臂 - Literally means 'covering the body with a garment,' indicating a protective action.
  • 與之共寢 - Literally means 'sleeping with it,' showing the narrator's willingness to share the bed.
  • 留燭以觀其變 - Literally means 'leaving the candle to observe its changes,' indicating the narrator's curiosity.
  • 半夜狐欠伸 - Literally means 'in the middle of the night, the fox stretches,' describing the fox's movement.
  • 美哉睡乎 - Literally means 'what a beautiful sleep,' expressing the narrator's admiration.
  • 啟覆視之 - Literally means 'opening the cover to look at it,' indicating the narrator's action to see the creature.
  • 儒冠之俊人也 - Literally means 'a handsome man with a scholar's cap,' describing the fox's appearance.
  • 起拜榻前 - Literally means 'rising to bow before the bed,' indicating the fox's respectful gesture.
  • 謝不殺之恩 - Literally means 'thanking for the grace of not being killed,' expressing gratitude.
  • 我癖於曲蘖 - Literally means 'I am addicted to fermented grains,' indicating the narrator's fondness for alcohol.
  • 卿，我鲍叔也 - Literally means 'you are my Bo Shu,' referring to a trusted friend, similar to the historical figure Bo Shu.
  • 如不见疑 - Literally means 'if you do not doubt me,' expressing a request for trust.
  • 当为糟丘之良友 - Literally means 'you will be a good friend of the wine hill,' indicating a promise of friendship.
  • 曳登榻复寝 - Literally means 'pulling [the fox] onto the bed to sleep again,' showing the narrator's continued hospitality.
  • 无相猜 - Literally means 'do not suspect each other,' expressing a desire for mutual trust.
  • 生既醒，则狐已去 - Literally means 'when the narrator woke up, the fox had already left,' indicating the fox's departure.
  • 乃治旨酒一盛专伺狐 - Literally means 'then he prepared a special wine to wait for the fox,' showing the narrator's anticipation of the fox's return.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 浮三白 - A traditional Chinese custom where three cups of wine are drunk before going to bed, often associated with a specific ritual or practice.
  • 儒冠 - A scholar's cap, symbolizing education and refinement.
  • 鲍叔 - A historical figure known for his loyalty and trustworthiness, often used as a metaphor for a trusted friend.
  • 糟丘 - A metaphor for a place of excessive drinking, often used to describe a tavern or a place where people gather to drink.
